content:
# Numbers Chapter 12 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), p. 162 | Codex Vaticanus pa_0121
**Source Images:** `NUM_012_page_001.jpg`

---

**1** Miriam and Aaron spoke against Moses on account of the Cushite woman he had married -- for he had taken a Cushite wife.

**2** They said, "Has YHWH spoken only through Moses? Has he not spoken through us as well?" And YHWH heard it.

**3** Now the man Moses was more humble than any person on the face of the earth.

**4** Suddenly, YHWH said to Moses, to Aaron, and to Miriam, "Come out, the three of you, to the Tent of Meeting." So the three of them went out.

**5** YHWH came down in a pillar of cloud and stood at the entrance of the tent. He called out, "Aaron! Miriam!" -- and the two of them stepped forward.

**6** He said, "Listen carefully to my words. If there is a prophet among you, I, YHWH, make myself known to him in a vision. I speak with him in a dream.

**7** Not so with my servant Moses. He is entrusted with my entire household.

**8** With him I speak face to face -- openly, not in riddles -- and he beholds the very form of YHWH. How then did you dare to speak against my servant, against Moses?"

**9** YHWH's anger burned against them, and he departed.

**10** When the cloud lifted from above the tent -- there was Miriam, afflicted with a skin disease white as snow! Aaron turned toward Miriam and saw that she was diseased.

**11** Aaron said to Moses, "Please, my lord! Do not hold against us the sin we committed so foolishly.

**12** Do not let her be like a stillborn child, whose flesh is already half-consumed when it comes from its mother's womb!"

**13** Moses cried out to YHWH, "God -- please -- heal her!"

**14** YHWH said to Moses, "If her own father had spit in her face, would she not bear her shame for seven days? Let her be shut outside the camp for seven days, and after that she may be brought back in."

**15** So Miriam was shut outside the camp for seven days, and the people did not set out on the march until Miriam was brought back in.

**16** After that, the people set out from Hazeroth and camped in the wilderness of Paran.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 -- "the Cushite woman" (האשה הכשית / ha'ishah hakushit):** The Hebrew is emphatic: Miriam and Aaron attacked Moses specifically *because of* (עַל־אֹדוֹת) his Cushite wife. The term *Kushit* refers to a woman from Cush (the region south of Egypt, broadly corresponding to Nubia/Ethiopia). Whether this is Zipporah by another designation or a second wife is debated, but the narrative emphasis falls on the ethnic otherness of the woman as the trigger for their complaint. The Vaticanus LXX renders this as τῆς γυναικὸς τῆς Αἰθιοπίσσης ("the Ethiopian woman"), confirming the identification with Cush/Ethiopia. I preserved the double mention ("the Cushite woman he had married -- for he had taken a Cushite wife") because the Hebrew's repetition is rhetorically deliberate: it underscores that this is the real grievance, not the prophetic authority question that follows.

**Verse 2 -- The pivot from personal to prophetic:** The complaint shifts abruptly from Moses' marriage to a challenge of his unique prophetic status. This rhetorical pivot reveals that the Cushite wife was a pretext; the deeper issue was jealousy over Moses' singular relationship with YHWH. The Hebrew הֲרַק אַךְ ("Has he only...?") carries an indignant tone -- not a genuine question but a resentful accusation. I rendered this with the rhetorical force of "Has YHWH spoken *only* through Moses?" to capture the bitterness. The Vaticanus text matches: μὴ Μωυσῇ μόνῳ ἐλάλησεν κύριος; The chapter's final clause -- "And YHWH heard it" (וַיִּשְׁמַע יהוה) -- is ominous, a narrative aside that signals divine judgment is coming.

**Verse 3 -- "more humble than any person" (עָנָו מְאֹד מִכֹּל הָאָדָם):** The word *'anav* (עָנָו) means humble, afflicted, meek -- one who does not grasp at status or self-defense. This parenthetical about Moses is striking: it explains *why* Moses does not defend himself and sets up YHWH as the one who must intervene. Some scholars note this verse as evidence of later editorial insertion, but its literary function is essential -- Moses' silence in the face of attack is the very quality that distinguishes him. The LXX renders πραΰς σφόδρα ("exceedingly gentle/meek"), reinforcing the non-retaliatory character.

**Verse 5 -- "Aaron! Miriam!" (אהרן ומרים):** YHWH calls them by name, summoning them out from behind Moses. The dramatic staging -- the cloud descending, the divine voice calling two names -- creates a courtroom scene. They are called as defendants. I rendered "the two of them stepped forward" to convey the solemn movement toward judgment, though the Hebrew simply says "the two of them went out" (וַיֵּצְאוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם).

**Verses 6-8 -- The prophetic hierarchy:** YHWH's speech is the theological center of the chapter. The contrast is sharp: ordinary prophets receive revelation through visions (מַרְאָה) and dreams (חֲלוֹם) -- mediated, symbolic, indirect. Moses receives something categorically different: mouth-to-mouth speech (פֶּה אֶל־פֶּה), direct clarity (מַרְאֶה וְלֹא בְחִידֹת, "openly, not in riddles"), and the sight of YHWH's own form (תְּמֻנַת יהוה). The word *temunah* ("form/likeness") is extraordinary -- it is the same word used in Deuteronomy 4:12-15 where Israel is told they saw *no* form at Sinai. Moses alone is granted this. I rendered "face to face" for פֶּה אֶל־פֶּה (literally "mouth to mouth") because the dynamic meaning is unmediated intimacy. The concluding question -- "How then did you dare to speak against my servant?" -- captures the Hebrew's מַדּוּעַ לֹא יְרֵאתֶם ("Why were you not afraid?"), which I intensified to convey the weight of the divine rebuke.

**Verse 10 -- "afflicted with a skin disease white as snow" (מְצֹרַעַת כַּשָּׁלֶג):** The Hebrew *metsora'at* refers to the condition commonly translated "leprosy" but more accurately denoting a range of severe skin conditions that rendered one ritually impure (see Leviticus 13-14). The punishment falls on Miriam alone, not Aaron -- a disproportion that has generated much commentary. The most likely reading is that Miriam was the instigator (she is named first in verse 1, and the opening verb וַתְּדַבֵּר is feminine singular in the Hebrew). Aaron's priestly status may also have shielded him, since a leprous priest could not serve. The simile "white as snow" (כַּשָּׁלֶג) emphasizes the totality and visibility of the affliction. The Vaticanus LXX reads λεπρῶσα ὡσεὶ χιών ("leprous as snow").

**Verse 12 -- "like a stillborn child" (כַּמֵּת):** Aaron's plea is visceral. The Hebrew compares Miriam to a *met* -- a dead thing -- specifically a stillborn whose flesh is already half-decayed at birth. The image is horrifying and deliberately so: Aaron is begging Moses to see what the disease is doing to their sister. The phrase אֲשֶׁר בְּצֵאתוֹ מֵרֶחֶם אִמּוֹ וַיֵּאָכֵל חֲצִי בְשָׂרוֹ ("when it comes from its mother's womb with half its flesh consumed") evokes death-in-life. I rendered "stillborn child" to make the comparison immediately intelligible while preserving the horror.

**Verse 13 -- "God -- please -- heal her!" (אֵל נָא רְפָא נָא לָהּ):** This is one of the shortest and most powerful prayers in the Hebrew Bible -- five words in Hebrew. The particle *na* ("please") appears twice, framing the urgent verb *refa* ("heal"). Moses, the one wronged, intercedes for the one who wronged him. The brevity is the point: no elaborate liturgical formula, just raw pleading. I preserved the staccato rhythm with dashes to convey the breathless urgency. The LXX renders ὁ θεός, δέομαί σου, ἴασαι αὐτήν ("O God, I beg you, heal her").

**Verse 14 -- "If her own father had spit in her face" (וְאָבִיהָ יָרֹק יָרַק בְּפָנֶיהָ):** YHWH's response uses a *qal wahomer* argument (lesser to greater): if even a human father's act of shaming required seven days of disgrace, how much more does divine displeasure demand a period of separation? The act of spitting in the face was a severe gesture of rejection and humiliation in the ancient Near East (cf. Deuteronomy 25:9, Isaiah 50:6). YHWH grants the healing Moses requested but insists on the seven-day quarantine -- justice and mercy held together. The verb יָרֹק יָרַק uses the infinitive absolute construction for emphasis ("had truly spit" / "had actually spit"), which I rendered as "her *own* father" to convey the intensification.

**Verse 15 -- The people wait:** The entire community halts its march until Miriam is restored. This detail reveals both the severity of the situation (the whole nation is affected by one person's sin and punishment) and Miriam's stature -- the people will not move on without her. It echoes the way Miriam once waited by the Nile to watch over the infant Moses (Exodus 2:4); now Israel waits for her.
